name: Global Intelligence — Aspect Taxonomy
notes: Phase 1 = global lens only: 12 categories, 41 core aspects (confirmed by Bhim 2026-07-11).
An aspect = category x topic x region; region lenses (US/EU/India/China...) come in a later phase.
v1.1 (2026-07-12): keywords are matched with WORD-BOUNDARY regex (not substring) against
lowercased item title+summary — 'gold' no longer matches inside 'golden'. A match requires
score >= 1.5 (a single title hit = 3.0 qualifies alone; a single summary-only hit = 1.0 does
not — avoids filing on one weak, out-of-context mention). Optional 'exclude_keywords' per
aspect (same word-boundary matching) zeroes out a match regardless of keyword score — used
for known collisions (e.g. 'gold' vs 'Golden Jubilee').
influences are the decision domains a briefing card surfaces: investments | daily-life | travel | business.
v1.2 (2026-07-19): audited keyword lists for Western/US-centric framing gaps. Fixed a real bug —
climate.policy's 'cop3' literally referred to COP3 (Kyoto, 1997), a keyword that could never
meaningfully match current news; replaced with cop28/29/30/31 + 'climate summit' (COP is hosted
in a different country/region each year — Dubai, Baku, Belem — so this directly serves global
coverage too). Added pboc/people's bank of china to economy.central-banks (only major central
bank missing by name), byd/tata motors/hyundai to business.autos-aviation, and
alibaba/tencent/huawei/bytedance/baidu to technology.big-tech (was 7 US companies + Samsung only).
v1.3 (2026-07-19): a UI-code audit of the wider app found travel.advisories' keyword list only
recognized the US State Department's own name for its advisories ('state department warning'),
with no equivalent for any other country's travel-advisory body. Added 'foreign office warning'
and 'fcdo advisory' for the UK's Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office — the next most
commonly cited advisory source in English-language coverage after the US State Department.
v1.4 (2026-07-20): a full keyword-list re-read (every aspect, not just the ones already known
to have gaps) found 3 more Western-institution-only keyword sets. technology.semiconductors
named TSMC/Nvidia/Intel/ASML but not Samsung or SK Hynix, 2 of the world's largest chipmakers —
added both. geopolitics.defense named only the Pentagon by institution; added 'pla'/'people's
liberation army' (China's military, frequently referenced by that acronym in English coverage).
technology.ai named OpenAI/Anthropic/DeepMind but not DeepSeek, whose January 2025 model
release triggered a real global market selloff — a concrete, high-impact omission, not just a
diversity gap. science-health.medicine named only the FDA; added 'ema approval' for the EU's
European Medicines Agency, the 2nd most commonly cited drug regulator in English coverage.
v1.5 (2026-07-20): 3rd pass, checking every remaining aspect not yet re-read for this specific
skew. technology.space named only NASA and SpaceX by institution — added 'isro' (India's space
agency, whose Chandrayaan-3 lunar landing was major global news) and 'roscosmos' (Russia's).
Reviewed markets.stocks/bonds/currencies, economy.*, business.*, climate.*, society.*, travel.*,
food-agriculture.* and found them already reasonably diverse (Nikkei/FTSE/Sensex/Hang Seng in
markets.stocks; gilt/bund alongside treasury in markets.bonds; Suez/Panama Canal in supply-
chains) — no further changes needed there this pass.
v1.6 (2026-07-21): after the depth-hunting source-registry arc (rounds 163-170) closed, a fresh
taxonomy re-read for institution-name skew found geopolitics.diplomacy named only G7/G20/NATO —
all Western/US-aligned multilateral bodies — with no equivalent for the major non-Western blocs
that host their own regularly-covered diplomatic summits. Added brics (now a significant
economic/geopolitical counterweight bloc following its 2024 expansion), african union, asean,
arab league, and shanghai cooperation organisation/sco summit.
v1.7 (2026-07-21): re-audited markets.currencies/stocks/bonds and economy.central-banks, which
v1.5 had checked lightly and called 'already reasonably diverse' — a closer pass found real
gaps. markets.currencies had no ruble, a striking omission given sustained Russia sanctions/war
coverage; added ruble, peso, brazilian real, naira, south korean won, south african rand, and
turkish lira (multi-word phrases used for won/rand specifically to avoid word-boundary collisions
with the common English words 'won' and the name 'Rand'). economy.central-banks named only 6
institutions (Fed/ECB/BOJ/BOE/RBI/PBOC); added bank of korea, banco central do brasil, south
african reserve bank, and bank of russia. markets.stocks had Nikkei/FTSE/Sensex/Hang Seng but no
continental European or Latin American index; added dax, cac 40, shanghai composite (China's own
domestic index, distinct from the Hong Kong-based Hang Seng already listed), bovespa, and kospi.
markets.bonds had gilt/bund for UK/Germany but nothing for Japan, the world's largest government
bond market; added jgb and japanese government bond.
v1.8 (2026-07-21): a fresh full re-read of every aspect's keyword list (first since v1.7) found
politics.leadership only covered 'prime minister'/'president' by title — zero coverage for
chancellor (Germany/Austria), premier (China's head of government), or any constitutional
monarch/emir/sultan, despite the source registry including 12 outlets from Gulf monarchies
(Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait, Oman, Bahrain) alone. Tested candidate keywords against live
fetched items before committing (not just in theory) and this caught two real problems: bare
'premier' flooded with French 'premier' meaning 'first' ('premier tour', 'premier plan',
extremely common across this registry's many Francophone African sources) plus 'Premier League'
and generic-adjective English uses — 47 matches, nearly all false positives; narrowed to
'chinese premier'. Bare 'emir' collided with 'Emir' as a personal first name (a real hit was
'saxofonista Emir Santacruz', a jazz musician, not a head of state) — narrowed to 'emir of',
which still matches the natural 'Emir of Qatar'/'Emir of Kuwait' phrasing but not a bare name.
'chancellor' and 'sultan' tested clean (Haiti's foreign minister and UK's Chancellor of the
Exchequer both correctly matched under 'chancellor'; Malaysian/Bruneian sultans matched cleanly).
Added the specific phrases 'king charles' and 'hong kong chief executive' rather than bare
'king'/'monarch'/'chief executive', since those bare forms collide heavily with unrelated
entertainment/sports references ('The Lion King', 'King James') and, for 'chief executive'
specifically, with the extremely common 'chief executive officer' (CEO) business usage that
would otherwise flood this political-leadership aspect with earnings/M&A stories.
v1.9 (2026-07-21): applied v1.8's empirical false-positive-testing technique to the *existing*
keyword set, not just new additions — a sweep never done systematically before. Fetched live
items and inspected actual matches for several structurally ambiguous bare-word keywords.
Confirmed real false positives in three: 'strike' (geopolitics.conflicts) caught cricket's
'strike-rate' batting statistic ('Tilak Varma hits back at strike-rate criticism'); 'attack'
(geopolitics.conflicts) caught animal attacks (bear/tiger/bison) and a football 'striker'
signing; 'campaign' (politics.elections) caught an unrelated pharma ad-campaign lawsuit, a
cricket-league 'campaign' (season), and an environmental tree-planting campaign. Only fixed the
one with a clean, complete, zero-recall-cost solution: added 'strike rate'/'strike-rate' as
exclude_keywords on geopolitics.conflicts — an unambiguous cricket/baseball statistics term with
no legitimate conflict-news overlap. Left 'attack' and 'campaign' unfixed: their false positives
come from diffuse, open-ended categories (dozens of animal species; ad/sports/environmental
campaign usage) that a small exclude_keywords list couldn't meaningfully cover without becoming
whack-a-mole busywork — documented here as a known, low-severity limitation rather than churned
on with a partial, incomplete fix.
